title: Digital Services Security Controls
verbatim: Securing digital services is an integral part of financial institution’s risk management. A financial institution must expand its CRF to implement robust technology security controls in providing digital services which assure the following: (a) adopt and regularly assess the minimum-security controls for respective delivery channels to ensure confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty information and transactions. Refer to Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11; (b) proper authentication of users or devices and authorization of transactions to mitigate impersonation and fraud risk. Refer the minimum-security controls in Appendix 3; and (c) strong physical control and logical control measures.
translation: All digital channels — internet banking, mobile banking, and payment systems — must have comprehensive security controls covering authentication, authorisation, encryption, session management, and protection against web application attacks.
evidence: Security control implementation evidence per channel

artifacts.name: Digital Channel Security Standards
artifacts.description: Standards defining security requirements for all digital channels

artifacts.name: Security Architecture Documentation
artifacts.description: Security architecture documentation for digital banking channels

artifacts.name: Digital Channel Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Security assessment reports for digital channels

title: Alternative Controls and Liability
verbatim: In the event that a financial institution has not yet implemented the digital services security controls specified under paragraph 12.1 to 12.9, the financial institution must– (a) be ready to provide documented explanation of how alternatives measures or mitigations achieve equivalent or superior effectiveness; and (b) assume the liability of any fraud that occurs due to the gaps arise from the absence of the specified control.
translation: Mobile banking apps must include security features like code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, and secure local storage. Test the security of each release before publishing to app stores.
evidence: App store release security sign-off records

artifacts.name: Mobile App Security Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for mobile application security including required security features

artifacts.name: Mobile App Security Test Reports
artifacts.description: Security testing reports for mobile application releases

artifacts.name: Mobile Security Feature Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of implementation of required mobile security features

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: CRF Enhancement for Digital Fraud
verbatim: The complex and fast evolving digital fraud requires financial institutions to be vigilant against new fraud techniques and proactive in strengthening their cyber defence for customer protection. In line with paragraphs 11.2 and 11.3, a financial institution must enhance its CRF as follows: (a) expand the scope of identification of cybersecurity threats and countermeasures to include customers’ mobile devices and access points; (b) adopt layered (defense-in-depth) security controls to protect the digital service application deployed to customers’ mobile devices and the relevant banking data contained in it; (c) perform continuous surveillance and monitoring to detect any exploitation of the digital service application deployed to customers’ mobile devices and ensure the swift upgrade of security controls to mitigate new vulnerabilities; (d) establish clearly defined and effective incident handling procedures to assist customers to contain the potential damage resulting from a cybersecurity breach involving digital services; (e) formalise operational arrangement to enable swift coordinated response and rapid upgrade of countermeasures to defend against advanced fraud tactics if a financial institution relies on multiple business functions; (f) conduct regular review by senior management to ensure the effectiveness of digital fraud management and define threshold for escalation of countermeasures considering the actual impact to victims of digital fraud and emerging fraud environment; and (g) apprise the board on the outcome of management reviews to preserve public confidence in the security of digital services and mitigate reputation risk to the industry.
translation: Secure all APIs with authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption. Maintain an inventory of all APIs, document them, and test their security regularly. Shut down deprecated APIs promptly.

artifacts.name: API Security Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for API security including authentication, rate limiting, and input validation

artifacts.name: API Inventory
artifacts.description: Complete inventory of all APIs with security classification and documentation

artifacts.name: API Security Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Regular security assessment reports for APIs

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Fraud Detection Capabilities
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that its fraud detection capabilities and rules are updated in a timely manner upon detection of new fraud modus operandi in order to prevent fraudulent transactions or account takeover using stolen customer credentials. This must be supported by appropriate risk analytics to improve the accuracy of fraud detection, that includes continuous upgrade of fraud detection capabilities as specified in Appendix 11 on Fraud Detection Standards.
translation: Customer authentication for digital services must match the risk level of the activity. High-risk transactions require MFA. Regularly assess whether your authentication mechanisms are still effective against current threats.

artifacts.name: Customer Authentication Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining authentication requirements based on transaction risk

artifacts.name: Authentication Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for risk-based and multi-factor customer authentication

artifacts.name: Authentication Assessment Report
artifacts.description: Assessment report on effectiveness of customer authentication mechanisms

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Digital Service Delivery Risk Mitigation
verbatim: A financial institution must mitigate any attendant risk arising from its delivery of digital services. This shall include– (a) adopt secure communication channel to mitigate risk of phishing. Compensating controls shall be adopted when using communication channel prone to phishing exploits; (b) all customers must be properly informed in advance of new controls to ease adoption and minimise inconvenience; and (c) practical ways for customers to verify the authenticity of calls made by the financial institution or its appointed outsourced service providers.
translation: High-value and high-risk digital transactions need out-of-band verification — confirming the transaction through a separate channel from the one used to initiate it. Show customers the full transaction details for confirmation before final authorisation.

artifacts.name: Transaction Verification Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for out-of-band verification of high-value and high-risk transactions

artifacts.name: Verification Mechanism Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration of independent channel transaction verification mechanisms

artifacts.name: Transaction Flow Documentation
artifacts.description: Documentation of transaction verification flow showing independent channel

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Fraud Risk Empowerment
verbatim: A financial institution shall empower customers to mitigate digital fraud risks where it is reasonably practical. This shall include offering customer mechanisms to manage activation and de-activation of payment card for card-not-present and overseas transactions. This shall not absolve the financial institution from its liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care to ensure the security of its digital services.
translation: Implement secure session management for digital services: automatic timeouts, securely generated session tokens, protection against session fixation attacks, and controls on concurrent sessions. Never put session tokens in URLs and always invalidate them on logout.

artifacts.name: Session Management Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for secure session management in digital services

artifacts.name: Session Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for session timeouts, tokens, and concurrent session controls

artifacts.name: Session Security Test Results
artifacts.description: Security testing results for session management controls

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Fraud Awareness Programmes
verbatim: Consumer competency is essential to strengthening the security of digital services. A financial institution must maintain continuous efforts to review and enhance the effectiveness of its awareness programmes, ensuring customers understand the risks of digital services fraud. This shall include– (a) continuous and timely updates of practical information on how to identify potential fraud, including specific information about new or common modus operandi; (b) clear explanation about new and existing security measures, such as how to verify genuine e-banking websites and mobile applications; (c) real-time alerts of possible risks when security measures are absent or have not yet been implemented; and (d) measures to further improve customer understanding and familiarisation with fraud risks and controls, such as through interactive simulations of security features etc.
translation: Deploy real-time fraud detection for digital transactions using behavioural analytics, pattern analysis, and anomaly detection. Flag suspicious transactions for review and be able to block them in real time when necessary.
evidence: Fraud detection system architecture and configuration
Fraud detection rules and models documentation
Fraud alert monitoring and investigation records

artifacts.name: Fraud Detection Architecture
artifacts.description: Architecture and configuration of real-time fraud detection systems

artifacts.name: Fraud Investigation Procedures
artifacts.description: Procedures for investigating flagged transactions and suspected fraud

artifacts.name: Fraud Detection Metrics Report
artifacts.description: Report on fraud detection rates, false positive rates, and effectiveness metrics

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Account Suspension and Kill Switch
verbatim: A financial institution must provide convenient means for customers to report, suspend and re-activate their account swiftly in the event of a suspected fraud, where applicable. This shall require the financial institution to– (a) offer a secure self-service "kill switch" solution; (b) ensure that its contact centre is adequately resourced and operating effectively to provide prompt and adequate assistance to customers in distress; and (c) restore customer access to digital services within a reasonable timeframe upon validation.
translation: When onboarding customers digitally, use security controls like identity verification, liveness detection, document checks, and fraud screening. The level of controls should match the risk of the products being offered.
evidence: Liveness detection and document verification evidence

artifacts.name: Digital Onboarding Security Standards
artifacts.description: Standards for security controls in digital customer onboarding

artifacts.name: Identity Verification Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of identity verification, liveness detection, and document checks

artifacts.name: Onboarding Security Assessment
artifacts.description: Assessment of digital onboarding security controls and effectiveness

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Post-Incident Customer Protection
verbatim: In addition to effective incident handling procedures, a financial institution must adopt additional measures to mitigate risk to customers arising from any incident involving potential fraud or compromise of customer data. This shall include– (a) heighten monitoring of affected customer accounts; (b) notify affected customers and provide them with the necessary information to apply mitigating measures and reduce the risk of fraud; and (c) revoke and re-issue affected user credentials or designated payment instruments, where there is a potential risk of exploitation due to compromised data or other fraudulent activity.
translation: Secure QR code payments and e-wallet services against tampering, overlay attacks, and forgery. Ensure QR codes are validated, transactions are encrypted, and the integrity and authenticity of every QR-based transaction is verified.
evidence: QR code and e-wallet security standards
Security testing results for QR and e-wallet features

artifacts.description: Standards for securing QR code payments and e-wallet services

artifacts.name: QR Security Implementation
artifacts.description: Evidence of QR code validation, encryption, and anti-tampering controls

artifacts.name: QR/E-Wallet Security Test Report
